title: Chronic LUQ pain with opioid overdose and behavioral toxicity in an elderly male with complex cardiac history
doorChart: 72-year-old male — LUQ pain with chronic history and self-harm attempt in ED — ESI 2
chiefComplaint: Longstanding left upper quadrant pain and acute overdose behavior in the ED
pmh: Chronic pain; atrial fibrillation; heart failure; obesity; diabetes; prior thoracotomy; multiple vascular and respiratory comorbidities; substance use history including opioid use disorder with prior treatment

patientProfile.arrivalMode: brought in by triage or security after an overdose event in the ED
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I’ve had LUQ stabbing pain for years that never seems to go away. It’s been hard to control with my prescribed meds. Today I came to the ED after I took more of my meds to see if I could get relief and because I felt ignored by the team. I’ve had surgeries including a thoracotomy, and pain management has always been involved. I’ve had a few hospital visits before for this issue, and this time I felt the need to act out because the pain wouldn’t settle and I wanted care right away. I denied new illnesses like fever or shortness of breath, and I was focused on my pain and getting my pain meds adjusted. I initially became agitated, was placed on a psychiatric hold, and later required monitoring and de-escalation. I later became physically disruptive in the hallway, leading to restraints and a telepsychiatry evaluation. I reported that I had taken a large amount of my pain medications to prove a point and to get a quicker response. I has a long history of chronic pain and complex cardiac disease, including atrial fibrillation with intermittent episodes, and I am currently on a regimen for pain management. I denied active suicidal or homicidal intent at baseline, though the events escalated. I was upset about how my pain management had been approached and was seeking adjustments. I remained polite when a care-provider was present during some interviews but remained distrustful of the ED team during the initial assessment. I recognized that I needed care but felt overwhelmed by the system and feared losing control of my pain regimen.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Former smoker; history of alcohol use but currently not drinking; retired and living with caregiver; works or has worked as a long-haul driver; high burden of chronic illnesses affecting activities of daily living
patientProfile.medications: Duloxetine 60 mg daily; Gabapentin 300 mg three times daily; Oxycodone 5 mg and Percocet 10-325 mg as prescribed; Metformin; Lisinopril; Metoprolol; Furosemide; various others listed for chronic conditions
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Appears agitated and verbally combative at times, but cooperative when engaged; required de-escalation and security involvement during the admission; telepsychiatry involvement anticipated

physicalExam.general: Cooperative at times but irritable and combative at others; appears his stated age but overall frail with multiple chronic illnesses
physicalExam.heent: Oropharynx moist; mucous membranes not dry; no acute trauma noted
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm with variable rate due to atrial fibrillation; no acute murmurs or rubs observed
physicalExam.respiratory: Breath sounds clear bilaterally; no wheezes or crackles; normal work of breathing
physicalExam.abdomen: LUQ abdominal wall pain described; abdomen soft, nondistended, nondistinct tenderness per patient report; no peritoneal signs noted on exam
physicalExam.neurological: Oriented to person, place, and date; no focal neuro deficits noted during stable periods; otherwise fluctuating mental state due to agitation and medical treatments
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: No acute deformities observed; patient mainly restless and attempting to move
physicalExam.skin: Skin warm and dry; no rashes or ulcers noted

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: WBC 5.8, Hgb not listed, Hct 41.0, RDW 14.8

labs.name: Metabolic panel, comprehensive
labs.result: Glucose 164 (elevated); all other components within normal limits

labs.name: Ethanol, blood
labs.result: Not elevated (noted in panel, not clinically significant)

labs.name: Rapid influenza A/B, RSV, and COVID-19 panel
labs.result: Not acutely infectious at time of testing

imaging.name: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis
imaging.result: No acute intra-thoracic or intra-abdominal acute process. Left thoracotomy changes with chronic left ribs fractures; soft tissue changes on the left anterolateral chest wall consistent with prior surgery/granulation tissue; no focal fluid collection; pulmonary artery enlargement suggesting pulmonary hypertension; indeterminate left subcarinal lymph node; stable left para-aortic lesion; hepatic steatosis.

goldStandard.presentation: A 72-year-old man with a long-standing history of left upper quadrant neuralgia/pain, likely related to prior thoracic surgery and intercostal nerve irritation, presents with acute agitation and an opioid overdose episode after self-administering a large amount of his prescribed analgesics. He has a background of chronic heart disease including atrial fibrillation with previous thoracotomy and postoperative rib fractures. On arrival, he reported that his LUQ pain had been persistent for years and not adequately controlled by his pain management plan, and he sought escalation of analgesia. During the ED course, he became acutely disruptive with unsafe behaviors, resulting in psychiatric holds and later active restraints, with ongoing telepsychiatry evaluation planned. He denied convincing evidence of acute cardiac ischemia on ECG and CT, with mild hemodynamic stability between agitation episodes.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Acute on chronic left upper quadrant pain likely due to intercostal neuralgia; opioid intoxication/overdose with agitation and psychiatric hold in the ED; comorbid chronic heart disease with atrial fibrillation; no acute cardiopulmonary catastrophe identified on imaging at this time
goldStandard.disposition: Discharged from ED with follow-up recommendations for pain management with his treating pain specialist; instructions to return immediately for new or worsening symptoms; arranged telepsychiatry evaluation and outpatient psychiatric/pain follow-up; discharged with plan to coordinate with his pain management team for adjusted regimen
goldStandard.mdm: The patient presented with a chronic pain syndrome complicated by an acute opioid overdose and behavioral instability. Given his long history of opioid use and reported nonadherence to pain management, the team weighed the risks of additional narcotics against potential benefits. CT findings did not show acute pathology explaining the pain and overdose, but revealed chronic postsurgical changes and chronic rib fractures. Cardiac rhythm was intermittently abnormal due to known atrial fibrillation but without acute ischemia on ECG and imaging. After therapeutic de-escalation, monitoring, and discussion with pain management and psychiatry, a discharge plan emphasizing continuity of care with his pain physician and outpatient telepsychiatry was pursued to avoid recurrence of unsafe behaviors and to optimize pain control. The patient was informed of risks, provided discharge instructions, and consented to outpatient follow-up; the care team documented milestones and safety checks throughout the stay.

title: Dehydration with Syncope in a 67-Year-Old Female
doorChart: Female, 60s — vomiting, diarrhea, and fainting episodes — ESI 3
chiefComplaint: nausea with vomiting and diarrhea with fainting spells
pmh: Hypopituitarism on hormone replacement (thyroid and steroids), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GERD; remote pituitary tumor history; smoker (former).

patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I started feeling unwell Friday evening with nausea and multiple bouts of vomiting. By Saturday I developed watery diarrhea and abdominal discomfort that has been constant but varying in intensity. I also had two fainting episodes, one yesterday and one today, each lasting only seconds and followed by quick recovery. I haven’t been able to keep anything down or drink much fluids. I had Huevos Rancheros on Friday before symptoms began. I’ve been taking my usual thyroid and steroid meds but missed a couple doses due to the vomiting. No fever or chest pain. No blood in stool or urine.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Former smoker; drinks alcohol occasionally; widowed; lives with family; retired.
patientProfile.medications: Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; hydrocortisone 15 mg daily; amlodipine 5 mg daily; calcium/vitamin D; statin; fish oil
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Cooperative, anxious about symptoms, wants to stay hydrated and understand plan

physicalExam.general: Alert, oriented, lying comfortably, not in acute distress
physicalExam.heent: oropharynx moist, mucous membranes slightly dry
physicalExam.cardiovascular: regular rate and rhythm; no murmurs; distal pulses intact
physicalExam.abdomen: soft, mild diffuse tenderness without rebound or guarding
physicalExam.neurological: GCS 15, cranial nerves II-XII intact, no focal deficits

labs.name: CMP, comprehensive
labs.result: Sodium 131; Potassium 3.5; Chloride 96; CO2 23; BUN 28; Creatinine 1.3; Glucose 93; Calcium 8.0; Albumin 3.9
labs.flag: borderline abnormal sodium; mild renal insufficiency

imaging.name: ECG 12-lead
imaging.result: Normal sinus rhythm, no acute ischemic changes

goldStandard.presentation: A 67-year-old woman with a history of hypopituitarism on chronic steroids and thyroid replacement presented with several days of nonbloody vomiting, ongoing diarrhea, diffuse abdominal discomfort, and two brief syncope events. Initial evaluation showed mild dehydration with laboratory evidence of prerenal azotemia and hyponatremia. Cardiac monitoring and serial vitals were performed given syncope history; troponins were repeatedly negative and no arrhythmia was detected. Abdominal symptoms were attributed to gastroenteritis with good response to IV fluids. She was clinically improving and was discharged home with outpatient follow-up and hydration plan.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Dehydration with hyponatremia, syncope likely dehydration-related; transient mild renal impairment; viral gastroenteritis
goldStandard.disposition: Discharged home from observation after clinical improvement with IV hydration and antiemetics; instructions given on hydration, electrolyte management, and return precautions; outpatient follow-up with primary care and endocrinology arranged
goldStandard.mdm: The patient arrived with symptoms consistent with volume depletion and possible dehydration-induced syncope. Given several hours of hydration and lack of fever, leukocytosis, or signs of systemic infection, the working diagnosis favored viral gastroenteritis with dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities (mild hyponatremia, prerenal azotemia). Cardiac causes of syncope were considered but serial troponins remained negative and ECGs showed no ischemia or arrhythmia. Cortisol was elevated, but there was no evidence of an adrenal crisis. The plan included continued observation if symptoms persisted, hydration with normal saline, antiemetics for nausea, and re-evaluation to determine need for admission. Ultimately, the patient stabilized, tolerating PO intake, and discharged with return precautions and outpatient follow-up.

title: Lymphoma History with Cough, Neck Swelling, and Wheezing in a Older Adult
doorChart: Male, 50s—cough, neck lump, and wheezing—ED CDU observation
chiefComplaint: cough with neck swelling and wheezing
pmh: History of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with prior treatments and transplant; ulcerative colitis status post colectomy; chronic medical issues including asthma, hypertension, kidney stones

patientProfile.arrivalMode: transported by ambulance to the ED and placed in CDU for observation
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I’ve been dealing with a two-week cough and a sore throat kind of felt like a cold family members were sick too. Early today I noticed a lump on the right side of my neck that started hurting last night and this morning the left side looked bigger as well. I don’t have fever or trouble breathing at rest, no chest pain, and I don’t have trouble swallowing. This is similar to a prior lymphoma episode but I’m told this neck swelling could be infection around a gland or a swollen lymph node. I’ve been in a clinical trial for chemotherapy in the past but not recently treated for months. My family has been sick, so I was worried this could be an infection plus concern for my cancer history.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Former smoker, occasional alcohol use, married, lives with spouse
patientProfile.medications: OC- listed in med rec: antibiotics for sinus symptoms, prior steroids, inhaled therapies; current home meds not fully listed here
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Tolerant but anxious about his current symptoms, cooperative with care, consistently documenting that neck swelling is tender but not severely alarming

physicalExam.general: Alert, appears well-nourished, in no acute distress
physicalExam.heent: Right neck mass tender and firm, left neck mildly swollen; no trismus; oropharynx clear; nares without erythema; no exudates
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, normal heart sounds, peripheral pulses intact
physicalExam.respiratory: Lungs with diffuse wheezes but good air movement; no crackles; no signs of respiratory distress
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no masses, normal bowel sounds
physicalExam.skin: Warm and dry, no rashes or edema

labs.name: Metabolic panel
labs.result: Sodium 133 (135-145) | Potassium 3.1 (3.5-5.5) | Chloride 102 | CO2 24 | Glucose 119 (70-100) | BUN 14 | Creatinine 0.9 | Calcium 8.3 (8.5-10.5) | Albumin 2.9 (3.5-5.0) | Alk Phos 149 ( <130)

labs.name: EKG
labs.result: Sinus rhythm, rate ~90-100 bpm, no acute ischemic changes

imaging.name: Chest X-ray 2 views
imaging.result: Cardiac size normal; mediport in right chest with tip in the upper atrium; left hilar fullness possibly lymphadenopathy; lungs clear; no focal consolidation or effusion

imaging.name: CT neck with contrast
imaging.result: Soft tissue swelling around the right neck, with surrounding fat stranding; no definite abscess; scattered subcentimeter nodes; sinusitis on imaging; CT supports possible cellulitis vs gland infection; plan for serial exams and antibiotics

goldStandard.presentation: A middle-aged man with a history of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma presents with a two-week history of nonproductive cough and with acute development of palpable neck swelling on the right that spread to the left by morning. He reports no fever or dyspnea at rest. Exam shows a tender, indurated neck mass on the right with milder edema on the left, wheezing on lung exam, and otherwise stable vitals. CT neck shows no abscess but soft tissue swelling consistent with cellulitis or sialoadenitis; chest imaging is largely without acute findings aside from hilar prominence. Given cancer history and neck findings, oncology was consulted for potential evaluation, while airway and infection risk are monitored in CDU. Treatments include inhaled bronchodilators, a short course of steroids, and broad antibiotics for neck swelling; plan includes serial neck exams and observation in CDU, with discharge planning if improving.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Wheezing/asthma exacerbation; neck swelling likely cellulitis or sialadenitis in the setting of lymphoma history; sinusitis; hyponatremia and hypokalemia noted on CMP; baseline anemia from prior disease
goldStandard.disposition: Observation in the CDU with serial exams, bronchodilator therapy, steroids, and antibiotics; pending oncology input for lymphoma-related considerations; discharge home if stable after overnight observation with return precautions
goldStandard.mdm: This patient presents with wheezing and neck swelling in a background of treated lymphoma, making the differential broad but anchored by imaging suggesting cellulitis vs gland infection rather than abscess. He demonstrates no respiratory compromise, normal oxygenation after initial therapy, and improvement with nebulized therapy and steroids. Labs reveal mild hyponatremia and hypokalemia, anemia consistent with known history. The team elected CDU observation with ongoing bronchodilator therapy, IV/PO antibiotics for neck swelling, steroids, and oncology follow-up. Risk stratification favored outpatient disposition if symptoms stabilize, with clear instructions for red flags (fever, increasing neck pain, swelling, fever, or respiratory distress).

title: Geriatric Mechanical Fall with Chest/Hip Pain, Normal Acute Injury Workup
doorChart: Female, 80s — fall with left chest and hip pain — ESI 3
chiefComplaint: I tripped and fell, landed on my left side and hurt my chest and hip
pmh: History notable for diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, prior falls, hypothyroidism, depression, and myasthenia gravis (previously treated; no current crisis).

patientProfile.arrivalMode: brought to ED CDU for observation after initial evaluation
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I live in an assisted living facility. I fell while trying to stand up from a chair a short while ago. I landed on my left side and felt pain in my chest area and my hip. There was a scrape on my left elbow and a cut on my right shin. I don’t remember losing consciousness. I recall the whole event but I was worried because my chest hurt and I’m sore around my hip and pelvis. I’ve had problems with falls before and I wear an emergency alert necklace. They gave me some pain medicine and did imaging to see if there was a broken bone or something inside, and I’ve been monitored since.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Widowed, lives with assistance at home; prior smoking history, now non-smoker; limited mobility; relies on family with arrange transport when needed
patientProfile.medications: Metformin, levothyroxine, prednisone, imuran (azathioprine), mestinon, simvastatin, aspirin, lidocaine patch, albuterol inhaler
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Cooperative, but clearly uncomfortable with pain; softly spoken, frequently checking on staff and asking questions about recovery

physicalExam.general: An elderly woman in mild distress due to pain, alert and oriented, appears well-nourished for age
physicalExam.heent: Head atraumatic, pupils equal and reactive
physicalExam.respiratory: Breathing even and non-labored; lungs clear on auscultation; tenderness noted over left chest wall
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, nondistended, mild tenderness to palp over left flank/hip region
physicalExam.neurological: MAE, cranial nerves grossly intact, no focal deficits
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Left rib/chest wall tenderness; tenderness to palpation over left hip region; able to move joints with limited pain; no gross deformity; range of motion preserved
physicalExam.skin: Skin warm and dry; abrasion over left elbow; skin tear on left chest area and right shin noted

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC normal range; no acute infection

imaging.name: CT abdomen/pelvis
imaging.result: No acute traumatic injury identified; no pancreatic or intra-abdominal pathology

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality; no pneumothorax or consolidation

imaging.name: Pelvis/hip and left chest CT X-ray
imaging.result: No acute fracture demonstrated; localized hematoma/soft tissue injury suspected

imaging.name: Lower extremity ultrasound
imaging.result: Not indicated by imaging performed; no DVT identified

imaging.name: Urinalysis
imaging.result: No signs of acute infection; ketones mildly present (context of limited intake)

imaging.name: EKG
imaging.result: No acute ischemic changes

goldStandard.presentation:
An 83-year-old woman presents after a mechanical fall onto her left side with localized pain in the left chest wall and left hip. She denies loss of consciousness and head injury but has minor abrasions on the left elbow and right shin. Initial assessment shows stable vitals and the patient is alert and oriented. She is placed on observation with pain control and a plan for serial examinations, imaging to evaluate for fracture or organ injury, and early physical therapy evaluation. Imaging studies return without evidence of acute traumatic injury; there is a focal hematoma/soft tissue injury of the chest/hip region but no fracture or internal organ damage detected. The patient remains hemodynamically stable with adequate pain control and is discharged with outpatient physical therapy and return precautions, to be followed up with her primary team and caretaker.

diagnosis: Falls in the elderly with trauma limited to soft tissue injuries; no radiographic acute injuries identified.

disposition: Observation CDU with plan for routine pain management, PT evaluation, and eventual discharge home with a caregiver; patient discharged home with instructions and follow-up arrangements.

mdm: The patient is elderly and at high risk for recurrent falls. Given the absence of acute injuries on CT and radiographs, the management focused on analgesia, functional assessment, and PT planning. Serial examinations ensure there is no delayed injury. Disposition reflects safe discharge with supports in place for home safety and gait assistance.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Soft tissue injury with contusion/hematoma at left chest wall and left hip; no acute fracture or internal organ injury identified on imaging
goldStandard.disposition: Discharged home with caregiver support; prescriptions provided for analgesia; referrals for outpatient PT/OT; return precautions explained; arrange follow-up with PCP and neurology as indicated
goldStandard.mdm: Assessment favored nonoperative management of soft tissue injury after fall in an elderly patient. Continued monitoring for evolving symptoms was discussed. Pain control optimized with nonopioid and/or short-acting analgesics; PT evaluation arranged for functional mobilization; imaging reviewed to rule out fracture or organ injury; discharge planned with safety measures and caregiver involvement.

title: Headache with dizziness in a middle-aged man under neurology workup
doorChart: Male, 50s — ongoing headaches with lightheadedness and diplopia — ESI 3
chiefComplaint: ongoing headaches with lightheadedness, dizziness, and double vision
pmh: Diabetes mellitus, hypertension, sixth cranial nerve palsy; under neurology evaluation for possible CNS demyelination vs sarcoidosis

patientProfile.arrivalMode: ED arrival via EMS/transfer from ambulatory care
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I’ve been dealing with headaches for several months — they’re intermittent, throbbing, and now worse, making it hard to tolerate. I’ve had episodes of lightheadedness and dizziness that come with standing, sometimes with a droopy, off-balance feeling. I’ve also noticed some double vision and blurry vision at times. Neurology has been following me for possible demyelinating disease or sarcoidosis. Recently I had a neurology visit, and they wanted an MRI of brain and spine and an outpatient neuro-ophthalmology ref. In the ED I was given some IV fluids, Tylenol, and Prochlorperazine which helped a bit. I’ve had no fever, neck stiffness, or weakness, and I’ve had a few falls but no loss of consciousness. I’m here to figure out what’s going on and what to do next.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Never smoker; no alcohol or illicit drug use documented; lives with family
patientProfile.medications: Multiple chronic meds listed in prior records (diabetes, HTN, seizure related risk not noted)
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Cooperative but visibly uncomfortable, explaining symptoms in a straightforward manner

physicalExam.general: Appears non-toxic and alert, not in acute distress
physicalExam.heent: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Eyes with extraocular movements intact, mild diplopia not observed on exam; ears/nose/throat without discharge or swelling
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs or rubs
physicalExam.neurological: A&Ox4, cranial nerves intact on exam; strength 5/5 in all extremities; gait steady with assistance as needed; no focal deficits detected

labs.name: Basic metabolic panel
labs.result: Creatinine 1.60 (elevated); BUN 28; Na 139; K 4.0; Cl 102; CO2 24

imaging.name: ECG 12-Lead
imaging.result: Sinus rhythm, rate 97 bpm; no ST elevations or acute changes

imaging.name: MRI brain w/wo contrast and MRI spine w/wo contrast
imaging.result: Results pending at CDU; neurologic imaging planned to evaluate for demyelinating vs inflammatory process; prior neurology plan recommended MRI follow-up and neuro-ophthalmology referral

goldStandard.presentation: A 57-year-old man with a history of diabetes and hypertension, who is under neurology evaluation for possible CNS demyelinating disease versus sarcoidosis, presents with several months of headaches accompanied by lightheadedness, dizziness, and diplopia. He reports orthostatic-type symptoms, no fevers or infectious symptoms, and no focal neurologic deterioration at ED exam. Prior neurology notes advised MRI of brain and spine, neuro-ophthalmology referral, and vestibular rehab as part of multidisciplinary management. In the ED he is given IV fluids, acetaminophen, and antiemetic therapy with partial relief. Vital signs are stable, though creatinine is modestly elevated suggesting possible CKD/AKI in context of diabetes. Imaging studies are ordered; MRI studies are pending. The care plan involves neurology consultation, MRI studies, serial examinations, hydration, and symptom-directed therapy; admission to CDU for observation with MRI scheduling and outpatient follow-up planned.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Headache with lightheadedness/dizziness and diplopia in the setting of chronic neurologic disease workup; suspected neurosarcoidosis or demyelinating process; mild AKI on CKD background
goldStandard.disposition: Observation CDU with plan for MRI and neurology follow-up; patient eloped from CDU against advice
goldStandard.mdm: This patient presents with a chronic, evolving neurologic symptom complex (headache, dizziness, diplopia) in the setting of known CNS inflammatory disorders. The ED evaluation focuses on ruling out emergent etiologies (intracranial hemorrhage, infection, stroke) while pursuing MRI to assess for demyelinating disease or neurosarcoidosis. The mild creatinine elevation is monitored but not thought to be the primary driver of symptoms. Neurology has been engaged; neuroradiology imaging is prioritized. The patient tolerated IV fluids and antiemetic therapy; plan to observe in CDU and reassess after MRI results. Disposition was initially observation, but patient left against medical advice; no definitive inpatient admission completed.

title: Traumatic Abdominal Pain in a Renal Transplant Recipient After Motor Vehicle Collision
doorChart: “Male, mid-40s — lower abdominal pain after a car crash — ESI 3”
chiefComplaint: lower belly pain after a car crash
pmh: History notable for renal transplant, otherwise no chronic illnesses mentioned

patientProfile.arrivalMode: brought in by EMS after motor vehicle crash
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I was the front-seat passenger in a crash traveling at highway speeds. My abdomen started hurting after the impact, mainly in the lower right quadrant. I was conscious and the pain was persistent but not exploding in intensity. I was wearing a seat belt and did not lose consciousness. I’ve got a kidney transplant in the right lower quadrant, and I’ve had some back and neck pain, too. After initial evaluation in triage, imaging was pursued to check for injuries around my transplant and other organs. I’ve been told I could go home if I improve.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Non-smoker, no alcohol or illicit drug use documented; lives with family
patientProfile.medications: Immunosuppression therapy for renal transplant (specifically not detailed here)
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Cooperative but anxious about the transplant and potential injuries

physicalExam.general: Apparent relief of distress after initial care, awake and oriented
physicalExam.heent: Head atraumatic, pupils equal and reactive
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal heart sounds, distal pulses palpable
physicalExam.respiratory: Clear lungs, no wheezes, no respiratory distress
physicalExam.abdomen: Tenderness to palpation in the right lower quadrant with guarding; no rebound; abdomen soft elsewhere
physicalExam.neurological: Alert and oriented; no focal deficits
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Midline cervical tenderness noted; back and neck pain reported; no gross deformity on exam
physicalExam.skin: Warm, dry, no sweating or diaphoretic state

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: Hgb 12.9 g/dL, WBC 14.8 x10^9/L, platelets 238 x10^9/L

labs.name: CMP
labs.result: Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, electrolytes within normal limits

imaging.name: XR Chest
imaging.result: No acute cardiopulmonary disease; no focal consolidation; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette

imaging.name: CTA Chest/abdomen/pelvis
imaging.result: No acute traumatic injuries identified; stable transplant kidney with patent vasculature; no free intraperitoneal fluid or organ rupture; uncomplicated by vascular injury or perinephric bleed

imaging.name: Renal transplant ultrasound (RU)
imaging.result: Normal vascular flow to transplanted kidney; no hydronephrosis; patent renal artery and vein; cortex and medulla normal for allograft

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: No acute traumatic injury detected in chest/abdomen/pelvis; right lower quadrant renal transplant appears normal; no free intraperitoneal fluid; no solid-organ injury

imaging.name: CT Angio Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: No evidence of vascular injury or PE; kidneys/ureters appear without acute obstruction; stomach distension and hiatal hernia noted but not clinically limiting

goldStandard.presentation: A 45-year-old man with a history of renal transplant sustained a high-speed motor vehicle collision as a restrained passenger. He reported persistent tenderness in the lower right quadrant of the abdomen. Initial evaluation included a trauma consult and a FAST ultrasound which showed no intraperitoneal free fluid. He underwent comprehensive imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast, CT angio chest/abdomen/pelvis, and renal transplant ultrasound) demonstrating no acute abdominal injury, no hydronephrosis of the transplanted kidney, and no evidence of intra-abdominal bleeding. He remained clinically stable with pain controlled by IV analgesia and antiemetics, tolerated PO intake, and no signs of deterioration.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Acute abdominal pain after motor vehicle crash in a patient with a renal transplant; no acute traumatic injury identified on imaging
goldStandard.disposition: Discharged home with instructions for hydration, return precautions for worsening abdominal pain, fever, vomiting, or hemodynamic instability; outpatient follow-up with primary care in 1–2 weeks; provided with oral analgesics as needed
goldStandard.mdm: The patient was hemodynamically stable throughout observation, with persistent but non-worsening abdominal tenderness. Serial abdominal examinations remained benign. Imaging ruled out solid organ injury, vascular injury, and hydronephrosis of the renal transplant. Given stability, he was observed briefly with reassessment and then discharged with analgesia and return precautions. The plan included ensuring adequate oral intake, hydration, and instructions to seek care if symptoms progressed.

title: Elderly Woman with Left Lower Quadrant Pain and Transaminitis on Dialysis
doorChart: Female, 70s- to- 80s? — abdominal pain in left lower quadrant with elevated liver enzymes — ESI 3
chiefComplaint: LLQ abdominal pain with new liver test abnormalities
pmh: Longstanding diabetes mellitus type 2, coronary artery disease status post multiple bypass grafts, heart failure with preserved/diastolic function, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, end-stage renal disease on thrice-weekly dialysis, prior vascular access complications, prior pancreatitis, history of anemia

patientProfile.arrivalMode: brought to ED and admitted to CDU for observation
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I’ve had left lower belly pain that’s been nagging me for a couple of days. It’s a steady ache in the lower left quadrant, sometimes sharp with movement. I’ve also felt nauseated at times and had two episodes of diarrhea. I’m on dialysis three times a week and have a lot of vascular problems—my doctors worry about my heart and liver because of my kidney disease and prior heart disease. They did a CT and I was told there’s some fluid near the liver and changes in the liver that might be related to heart problems, not an infection. My abdomen hurts and it’s not getting much better, even with pain medicine. I’ve had similar visits before for this risk stuff, and they’re watching me closely with serial exams.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Married, has three children. Never smoker, no alcohol or illicit drug use. Lives with family. Retired professional; attends regular dialysis.
patientProfile.medications: Insulin regular, warfarin (coumadin) with recent INR elevation, midodrine, acetaminophen for pain, aspirin daily, various meds for CKD and CV disease
patientProfile.allergies: Risperdal causing altered mental status; latex allergy; thombocytopenia risk with transfusions
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Cooperative but appears fatigued and anxious due to persistent abdominal pain and multiple ongoing medical issues

physicalExam.general: A generally well-appearing woman, alert, not in acute distress at the moment.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm with a soft heart murmur acknowledged; distal pulses palpable.
physicalExam.respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation; no wheezes or crackles; comfortable breathing.
physicalExam.abdomen: Left lower quadrant tenderness to palpation without rebound or guarding; no distention or palpable masses noted.
physicalExam.neurological: Oriented to person and place; not oriented to date due to hospitalization context.
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: No noted edema or focal deficits in extremities.
physicalExam.skin: Shunt site shows a small amount of serosanguinous drainage; thrill present but diminished; no signs of infection.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: WBC 5.6 K/µL (normal); Hgb 10.5 g/dL (mild anemia); Hct 32.2% (low); MCV 102.3 fL (slightly high); Platelets 150-400 K/µL range; neutrophils 62%; lymphocytes 24%

labs.name: BMP/Metabolic panel
labs.result: Na 133 mmol/L (mild hyponatremia); K 3.5; Cl 93; CO2 29; BUN 18; Cr 4.76 mg/dL (elevated); eGFR 9 mL/min/1.73m2 (low); Glucose 119 mg/dL; Ca 8.5; Mg 1.9
labs.flag: abnormal for renal function and electrolytes

labs.name: Liver function tests
labs.result: AST 641 U/L; ALT 999 U/L; Alk Phos 215 U/L; Total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL; GGT not provided

labs.name: Troponin I
labs.result: Just above upper limit of normal initially; repeated later低

imaging.name: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.result: No acute diverticulitis; mild mesenteric fat stranding and perihepatic free fluid; subtle decreased enhancement of the ascending colon of unclear significance; suboptimal for mesenteric ischemia due to single-phase technique; hepatic “nutmeg” appearance suggesting congestive hepatopathy; comparison with prior CTs noted; left hepatic vein not clearly seen
imaging.flag: abnormal findings consistent with congestion; limited mesenteric ischemia evaluation

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.result: Patent hepatic vasculature; hepatic veins with pulsatile, high-resistance flow; small portal flow disturbance on left portal vein; trace perihepatic ascites; gallbladder absent; kidneys atrophic with ESRD changes; no biliary dilation
imaging.flag: abnormal flow pattern suggesting congestive hepatopathy vs Budd-Chiari considerations

imaging.name: ECG 12-lead
imaging.result: Sinus rhythm, no acute ischemic changes

goldStandard.presentation: A 72-year-old woman with long-standing vascular disease and ESRD on hemodialysis presents with two days of left lower quadrant abdominal pain, described as constant and variable in intensity, accompanied by intermittent nausea and two episodes of diarrhea. She has a complex cardiac history including prior CAD with bypass, heart failure risk, and chronic anticoagulation. Initial imaging shows no acute diverticulitis but demonstrates perihepatic fluid and a pattern of hepatic parenchymal heterogeneity (nutmeg liver) compatible with congestive hepatopathy. Serial exams and labs were planned due to concern for mesenteric ischemia given her chronic vascular disease, though the CT was limited by single-phase contrast. Elevated bilirubin and transaminases are noted, with INR elevation and mild hyponatremia; troponin is minimally uptrending, possibly CKD-related. The patient was admitted to the CDU for observation, serial abdominal exams, pain control, and hepatobiliary/cardiac evaluation, with GI consult requested and dialysis to continue with attention to electrolyte management and potential Budd-Chiari considerations. Disposition: admission to CDU for monitoring with planned repeat LFTs, lactate, troponin, and ongoing evaluation by GI and medicine services.
goldStandard.diagnosis: 1) Acute abdominal pain of left lower quadrant origin, 2) Acute hepatitis with marked transaminase elevation likely related to congestive hepatopathy in the setting of CKD and chronic liver/vascular disease, 3) ESRD on hemodialysis with complex vascular access
goldStandard.disposition: Admitted to Clinical Decision Unit for serial abdominal exams, pain control, and multidisciplinary evaluation (GI and medicine) with plans for repeat labs, hepatic ultrasound as indicated, and dialysis coordination
goldStandard.mdm: The patient’s abdominal pain in the setting of his long-standing vascular disease and ESRD raises concern for mesenteric ischemia; however, imaging did not reveal acute diverticulitis or clear bowel ischemia, and the study is limited by single-phase contrast. The prominent transaminase elevation and nutmeg liver pattern point toward congestive hepatopathy rather than infectious or obstructive etiologies. Given elevated INR and ongoing pain, the team pursued serial exams, repeat LFTs, lactate, and troponin, and initiated GI consultation to assess for Budd-Chiari syndrome and other hepatobiliary causes. Dialysis logistics were addressed, and the patient remained hemodynamically stable with no signs of acute abdomen requiring emergent surgical intervention. The plan emphasizes careful observation, interdisciplinary input, and reassessment for evolving mesenteric ischemia or hepatobiliary pathology.

title: Pulmonary Embolism in a Chronic GI and Autonomic Disorder Patient after Nerve Block
doorChart: Female, 30s — shortness of breath and chest/back pain — ESI 2
chiefComplaint: shortness of breath and chest/back pain after a nerve block
pmh: Autoimmune autonomic dysfunction (POTS) with gastroparesis status post gastric pacemaker, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, adrenal insufficiency; history of prior pulmonary embolism; chronic abdominal pain with GI interventions

patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I’m a 38-year-old woman with long-standing autonomic problems who recently had a nerve block for abdominal pain. Since the procedure I’ve been short of breath and have a headache that’s worse when I sit up and improves when I lie down. I’ve had a history of pneumothoraces after blocks. I normally use supplemental oxygen at home, and I’m not currently taking Lovenox. I’ve had LUQ abdominal pain radiating to my back and chest, and today I’ve felt chest pressure on and off, with back pain that’s been persistent for about a day and a half. I’ve been unable to ambulate well without discomfort. I’m worried about a blood clot given my prior PE and my vascular history, and the team is evaluating for PE, pneumothorax, or other causes of SOB.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Non-smoker; alcohol use infrequent; lives with family; takes multiple daily meds for chronic conditions; prior central venous access and multiple hospitalizations for GI/autonomic issues
patientProfile.medications: Home regimen includes partial gastric pacemaker therapy, ususal GI meds, BP/support medications; currently not taking Lovenox as previously used; listed PRN and chronic meds in chart
patientProfile.allergies: Betadine (topical) may cause hives; marinol can cause reactions in high doses per family history
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Calm but visibly anxious about breathing and chest discomfort; cooperative with clinicians; willing to undergo testing and management to rule out PE and other causes of SOB

physicalExam.general: Obese female, not in acute distress but anxious and short of breath
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, normal heart sounds, no murmurs or friction rub
physicalExam.respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no wheeze or rhonchi; breathing shallow but nonlabored at rest
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft, non-distended; LUQ tenderness noted on exam but without peritoneal signs
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: No focal deficits; gait steady while ambulating

labs.name: Metabolic panel, comprehensive
labs.result: CO2 21, BUN 23, Cr 0.81, K 5.3, Alk Phos 118, AST 37, ALT 19, GLC 100

imaging.name: XR Abdomen
imaging.result: Moderate fecal retention extending to near the ascending colon

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis w IV contrast
imaging.result: No acute abdominal abnormality; large stool burden suggesting constipation; borderline splenomegaly unchanged; cholelithiasis present

imaging.name: US Upper Extremity Veins (PICC area)
imaging.result: No flow in right mid basilic vein around the PICC, likely superficial thrombosis; chronic occlusion of right internal jugular vein

imaging.name: CT Chest Angiography w IV contrast - Pulmonary Embolism
imaging.result: Acute segmental pulmonary emboli in the right lower lobe lateral/posterior segments and the right upper lobe anterior segment; no evidence of right heart strain

imaging.name: XR Chest 2 Views
imaging.result: No significant interval change; no significant pneumothorax; low volumes with vascular markings possibly congestion or artifact

goldStandard.presentation: A 38-year-old woman with a complex history of autonomic dysfunction (POTS), Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, gastroparesis with gastric pacemaker, and prior pulmonary embolism presents after a recent left thoracic/transdiaphragmatic nerve block for abdominal pain. She reports progressive shortness of breath since the procedure, chest/back pain, and a headaches that worsen when upright. She has been off anticoagulation for weeks, uses home O2 at 2 L, and has a prior history of spontaneous pneumothorax. Initial assessment focused on ruling out PE, pneumothorax, ACS, and infectious etiologies. A CT pulmonary angiogram ultimately confirms acute segmental pulmonary emboli without signs of right heart strain. She also has chronic GI and splenic abnormalities and a measurable stool burden on CT consistent with constipation. Pain control is an important consideration given analgesic needs and cardiopulmonary safety.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Acute pulmonary embolism; constipation with stool burden; chronic splenomegaly; chronic abdominal pain with autonomic dysfunction; nonacute headache
goldStandard.disposition: Admit to medicine service for management of PE with anticoagulation planning and analgesia optimization; plan to continue heparin or other anticoagulation per protocol, monitor for bleeding, reassess tolerance of PO meds, and coordinate GI/pain management
goldStandard.mdm: This patient carries a high baseline thrombotic risk (prior PE, autonomic dysregulation, vascular abnormalities with EDS) and recently interrupted anticoagulation, elevating PE risk after a nerve block. The CT PE confirms segmental emboli in both lower and upper lobes without evidence of hemodynamic compromise. Given chronic GI complaints and potential for GI bleed, bleeding risk with anticoagulation is balanced against thrombosis risk. An initial heparin-based anticoagulation strategy with careful transition to an oral or longer-acting agent will be considered, with close monitoring, serial imaging or labs as indicated, and multidisciplinary input from medicine, GI, and anesthesia as the patient’s pain and respiratory status evolve. The patient’s prior pneumothorax history keeps pneumothorax high on the differential; imaging exclusions have been pursued. The immediate goals are stabilization, adequate pain control, and safe anticoagulation initiation while evaluating the need for further imaging in the setting of potential small pneumothorax or ongoing embolic risk.

title: Vertebrobasilar Vertigo with Transient Symptoms in a High-Risk Patient with End-Stage Renal Disease
doorChart: Female, 30s–40s — dizziness/vertigo episode with transient neurologic concerns — ESI 3
chiefComplaint: I woke up with dizziness that lasted about a minute and then went away
demographics: Female in her early 30s (shifted age to mid-30s)
pmh: Anemia; end-stage renal disease on dialysis; prior strokes; multiple vascular and oncologic history including prior liver lesions and metastatic risk; prior bone marrow transplant; on anticoagulation

patientProfile.arrivalMode: brought by EMS or walked into CDU for evaluation
patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I was resting in bed when I suddenly felt off-balance and dizzy for about 60 seconds this afternoon. It felt like previous strokes I’ve had with vertigo and nausea, though I stopped noticing any weakness or numbness as soon as it happened. By the time I reached the ED I was back to baseline and had no persistent dizziness or blurred vision. I did notice some mild shortness of breath, which could be from a shorter dialysis session yesterday. This is very similar to scenes from my past strokes, so I’m worried but hopeful it’s not another stroke.
patientProfile.socialHistory: Non-smoker; uses cannabis products recreationally; relies on caregiver for daily activities; lives with family
patientProfile.medications: Multiple chronic meds including anticoagulation; details provided in chart
patientProfile.allergies: Azithromycin — anaphylaxis; several drug allergies noted historically
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Cooperative but visibly anxious; concerned about recurrence of neurologic symptoms

physicalExam.general: Alert, oriented, in no acute distress during exam
physicalExam.heent: Pupils equal and reactive; otherwise nonfocal exam
physicalExam.respiratory: Clear lungs, no distress, occasional mild crackles at bases likely related to fluid balance
physicalExam.neurological: Baseline cranial nerves intact, no focal deficits on exam, gait not assessed due to baseline mobility limitations
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Evidence of prior amputations and vascular changes noted in extremities

labs.name: CMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 96, CO2 29, BUN 14, Cr 2.9 (elevated), eGFR 22

imaging.name: CT Head (non-contrast)
imaging.result: No acute intracranial process identified

imaging.name: CTA head/neck
imaging.result: No large vessel occlusion; no flow-limiting stenosis

imaging.name: MRI Brain (stroke protocol)
imaging.result: Scattered remote infarcts, most notable in left cerebellar hemisphere; no acute infarct identified; no new ischemia

goldStandard.presentation: A 33-year-old female with a long and complex medical history including end-stage renal disease on dialysis and prior cerebrovascular events presented for evaluation after a brief episode of vertigo and dizziness that began while at rest. The symptom resolved by ED arrival and exam was non-focal with no current neurologic deficits. Initial imaging with CT head was negative for acute injury, CTA showed no large vessel occlusion, and MRI brain demonstrated remote infarcts without an acute ischemic stroke. She remained hemodynamically stable and without ongoing neuro complaints, with plan for observation and outpatient neurology follow-up. The differential included TIA/stroke, syncope with CNS hypoperfusion, or vertebrobasilar insufficiency, but the majority of objective testing did not support an acute stroke. Disposition: discharged home with neuro follow-up and tiếp to resume baseline dialysis schedule; stroke precautions provided.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Transient ischemic attack/stroke evaluation with remote infarcts on MRI but no acute infarct and no large-vessel occlusion; vertigo/dizziness etiology likely non-acute cerebrovascular event in setting of complex chronic disease.
goldStandard.disposition: Observation CDU with MRI brain; discharge home with outpatient neurology and PCP follow-up; plan to continue dialysis as scheduled; no acute inpatient interventions required
goldStandard.mdm: The patient has a high-risk chronic illness burden (ESRD on dialysis, prior strokes, complex vascular disease) increasing her baseline risk for cerebrovascular events. Given an isolated brief dizziness/vertigo with full resolution and a normal neuro exam, the team pursued rapid imaging to exclude acute stroke. Non-contrast CT head was unrevealing, CTA showed no large vessel occlusion, and MRI revealed remote infarcts without acute ischemia. The NIHSS remained 0, and there were no focal deficits, supporting a low likelihood of evolving stroke. Neuro was consulted and recommended discharge with MRI follow-up and PCP/neuro coordination. The patient tolerated observation with stable vitals and no recurrent symptoms. Pain management needs were reassessed later in admission, and the patient expressed desire to go home with plan for continued outpatient care.

title: Severe polytrauma with open-book pelvic fracture and thoracic injuries after high-speed crash
doorChart: Male, 60s — blunt trauma after vehicle crash with ejected passenger — ESI 1
chiefComplaint: blunt trauma after a rollover crash with severe injuries
demographics: Shifted age to mid-60s (63 years old)

patientProfile.historyOfPresentIllness: I was the driver in a high-speed crash. I was ejected from the vehicle and saw significant damage to the car. I initially was alert but became progressively less responsive during transport. I developed trouble breathing with a drop in oxygen saturation and underwent needle decompression before I reached the ED. I remember being intubated in the field due to poor airway status and hypotension. In the ED they placed a chest tube on the left side and started resuscitation. I have had imaging showing an open book pelvic fracture, fractures of my right humerus, and suspected brain injury with intraventricular hemorrhage. I remain critically ill, with ongoing need for ventilatory and hemodynamic support.
patientProfile.personalityNotes: Very shaken, minimally responsive at arrival, intubated and heavily monitored

physicalExam.general: Sedated, intubated patient in the ED, critical condition
physicalExam.heent: Pupils equally reactive, left chest wall injury suspected
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic initially, now intermittently hypotensive requiring vasopressors
physicalExam.respiratory: Endotracheal tube in place, left chest tube in situ, right chest tube placed later; ongoing pulmonary contusions suspected; hypoxic with low oxygen saturations
physicalExam.abdomen: Soft to palpation? nondiagnostic due to intubation; abdomen soft, nondistended
physicalExam.neurological: GCS 3 per prehospital and ED notes; intracranial hemorrhage suspected with need for neurosurgical consult
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Open-book pelvic fracture with diastasis; comminuted right humeral fracture; various focal limb injuries
physicalExam.skin: No obvious external bleeding besides chest/pelvic injuries

labs.name: ABG
labs.result: pH 7.28, pCO2 48, HCO3 18, O2 sat 82% on ventilation

imaging.name: XR Chest
imaging.result: Left chest tube with tip near left apex; left pneumothorax decreasing but persistent basilar component; right pneumothorax minimal; contusions suspected

imaging.name: XR Pelvis
imaging.result: Open-book pelvic diastasis with pubic symphysis widening; SI joints widened; no definite displaced pelvic fracture seen on plain film

imaging.name: XR Humerus
imaging.result: Right humeral shaft fracture, comminuted

imaging.name: CT Chest/Abd/Pelvis w IV contrast
imaging.result: New pulmonary embolism extending from right pulmonary artery to lobar arteries with right heart strain suspected; bilateral contusions with left greater than right pneumothorax; sternal fracture with retrosternal hematoma; extraperitoneal Retzius-space hematoma; left iliac fracture with diastasis; multiple spine injuries including acute fractures from T12-L5; right humeral fracture; left orbital trauma with adnexal involvement; diffuse soft tissue injuries; left chest tube in place; bilateral chest tubes with differential position changes over time

goldStandard.presentation: 63-year-old male involved in a high-speed motor vehicle crash, unrestrained driver, ejected from the vehicle. Initial field management included needle thoracostomy due to suspected tension physiology and airway compromise. He arrives intubated with GCS 3, hypotensive and profoundly hypoxic. Imaging reveals an open-book pelvic ring injury with pubic diastasis and bilateral sacroiliac joint widening, a comminuted fracture of the right humerus, and multiple craniovertebral/spinal injuries with intraventricular hemorrhage and probable cervical spine ligamentous disruption. The patient develops bilateral thoracic injuries with contusions and pneumothoraces, and a pulmonary embolism is later identified. He remains critically ill in the ICU, requiring ongoing ventilatory and hemodynamic support, transfusions, and invasive monitoring. Final diagnoses include traumatic intracranial hemorrhage with loss of consciousness, acute traumatic injuries to the spine and pelvis, rib and sternum fractures with contusions, acute kidney injury/ATN in the context of hypovolemia, and vasopressor-dependent shock.
goldStandard.diagnosis: Traumatic injuries with open-book pelvic fracture; bilateral thoracic injuries with contusions and pneumothoraces; right humeral fracture; intraventricular hemorrhage with suspected spinal injury; acute kidney injury/ATN; pulmonary embolism; neurogenic/hypovolemic shock; need for emergent airway management and massive transfusion protocol.
goldStandard.disposition: Admitted to the ICU for continued aggressive resuscitation, airway management, surgical and neurosurgical consultations, and ongoing hemodynamic and ventilatory support. Plan includes repeat imaging, definitive management of pelvic and spinal injuries, chest tube management, and consideration of ECMO in refractory hypoxemia if available.
goldStandard.mdm: This is a classic polytrauma scenario with life-threatening injuries requiring immediate prehospital and in-hospital interventions. The patient presented with airway compromise and profound shock; immediate airway control (RSI), chest decompression and chest tube placement, and rapid massive transfusion were critical. Given open-book pelvic fracture with diastasis and sacroiliac joint widening, there was concern for ongoing pelvic bleeding, prompting interventional radiology involvement for embolization after stabilization. Chest imaging showed contusions and resolving pneumothoraces, but a new PE was identified, complicating management due to intracranial/spinal injuries restricting anticoagulation. The patient remained unstable in the ICU with neuro and respiratory failure, necessitating escalating support, invasive monitoring, and multidisciplinary coordination with trauma, neurosurgery, orthopedics, and critical care. The aggressive resuscitation and surgical interventions aimed to prevent further decompensation while planning definitive stabilization and rehab.
